questions.question:
Consider a network with 6 routers R1 to R6 connected with links having weights as shown in the following diagram.

[Network: R1 connects to R2 (weight 1), R2 connects to R4 (weight 2), R4 connects to R6 (weight 1), R6 connects to R5 (weight 2), R5 connects to R3 (weight 1), R3 connects to R1 (weight 3). Also R2 connects to R5 (weight 5), R1 connects to R4 (weight 4), R3 connects to R6 (weight 5), R2 connects to R3 (weight 3).]

Suppose the weights of all unused links are changed to 2 and the distance vector algorithm is used again until all routing tables stabilize. How many links will now remain unused?

A. 0
B. 1
C. 2
D. 3
questions.to_find: Number of links that remain unused after changing all unused link weights to 2 and re-running distance vector routing

understand.plain: A network of 6 routers with various weighted links is given. After running the distance vector algorithm, some links are not part of any shortest path and are considered 'unused'. The question asks: if we set all those unused links to weight 2 and run distance vector again, how many links still go unused? Since weight 2 is competitive with many existing path costs, most previously-unused links become attractive, and ultimately all links end up being used.

keywords.explain: A routing protocol where each router maintains a table of minimum costs to every destination, sharing vectors with neighbors iteratively until stable.
keywords.example: Router R1 learns the cheapest path to R5 by comparing all neighbors' reported costs plus the link cost to each neighbor.

keywords.explain: A link that is not part of the shortest path tree from any router to any other router after the routing tables stabilize.
keywords.example: If R2-R5 has weight 5 but the path R2-R4-R6-R5 costs 1+1+2=4, the R2-R5 link is unused.

keywords.explain: The state where no router updates its routing table further because all minimum costs are already known.
keywords.example: After several rounds of exchange, every router's distance vector stops changing.

understand.visual_alt: A network of 6 routers (R1-R6) arranged in a hexagonal pattern. Solid lines show the main ring links with given weights; dashed lines show additional cross-links. After distance vector stabilization, links with higher weights become unused. Changing unused link weights to 2 makes them competitive, resulting in all links being used.

given.aim: Find how many links remain unused after re-running distance vector with unused links set to weight 2

terms.meaning: 6 routers R1-R6 with a ring structure and several cross-links with given weights
terms.example: R1-R2 has weight 1, R2-R4 has weight 2, R4-R6 has weight 1, etc.
terms.connects: Determines which paths are shortest and which links are used

terms.meaning: Links not on any shortest path tree after the first stabilization
terms.example: R2-R5 (weight 5) is unused because R2-R4-R6-R5 = 4 is shorter
terms.connects: These links get their weights changed to 2 in the second phase

terms.meaning: All unused links are assigned weight 2, making them potentially competitive
terms.example: R2-R5 changes from 5 to 2, making it shorter than R2-R4-R6-R5 = 4
terms.connects: With weight 2, most previously-unused links become part of shortest paths

terms.meaning: Running distance vector again with updated weights until routing tables are stable
terms.example: Routers exchange updated distance vectors incorporating the new link weights
terms.connects: Determines the final set of used links

given.plan: First identify which links are unused after initial distance vector stabilization. Then set those links to weight 2 and re-run distance vector. With weight 2 being competitive (same as or less than many multi-hop path costs), check if any link still fails to be part of any shortest path.

steps.title: Identify unused links after first stabilization
steps.formula_raw: d(u,v) = min over all neighbors w of {cost(u,w) + d(w,v)}
steps.apply: Given the network, compute shortest paths. The ring path R1-R2-R4-R6-R5-R3-R1 with weights 1+2+1+2+1+3=10. Cross-links like R1-R4 (weight 4), R2-R3 (weight 3), R2-R5 (weight 5), R3-R6 (weight 5) are candidates to be unused. For example: R1 to R4: direct=4, via R2=1+2=3, so direct link R1-R4 is unused. R2 to R5: direct=5, via R4-R6=2+1+2=5 or R4-R6-R5=3 so direct might be unused. R2-R3: direct=3, via R1=1+3=4 or via R4-R6-R5-R3=2+1+2+1=6, so R2-R3 is used (3 < 4). After analysis, the unused links are R1-R4 (weight 4) and R3-R6 (weight 5) and R2-R5 (weight 5).
steps.note: A link is unused only if there exists a strictly shorter alternative path for every router pair that might use it.

steps.title: Change unused link weights to 2 and re-run distance vector
steps.apply: Set R1-R4=2, R3-R6=2, R2-R5=2 (previously unused links). Now re-check: R1 to R4: direct=2, via R2=1+2=3, so direct R1-R4 (w=2) is now used. R3 to R6: direct=2, via R5=1+2=3, so R3-R6 (w=2) is now used. R2 to R5: direct=2, via R4-R6-R5=2+1+2=5 or via R1-R3-R5=1+3+1=5, so R2-R5 (w=2) is now used. All previously unused links are now part of shortest paths. Therefore 0 links remain unused.
steps.note: Setting weight to 2 makes all previously-unused links competitive because the alternative multi-hop paths cost 3 or more.

questions.formula_note: Bellman-Ford shortest path update rule: d(u,v) = min_{w neighbor of u} {cost(u,w) + d(w,v)}. A link is used if it lies on at least one shortest path in the final stable routing tables.

questions.question:
Consider a network with five nodes, N1 to N5, as shown as below.

[Network: N1 connects to N2 (cost 1), N1 connects to N3 (cost 7), N2 connects to N4 (cost 7), N3 connects to N4 (cost 2), N3 connects to N5 (cost 6), N4 connects to N5 (cost 4), N2 connects to N5 (cost 3).]

The network uses a Distance Vector Routing protocol. Once the routes have been stabilized, the distance vectors at different nodes are as follows.
N1: (0, 1, 7, 8, 4)
N2: (1, 0, 6, 7, 3)
N3: (7, 6, 0, 2, 6)
N4: (8, 7, 2, 0, 4)
N5: (4, 3, 6, 4, 0)

Each distance vector is the distance of the best known path at that instance to nodes N1 to N5, where the distance to itself is 0. Also all links are symmetric and the cost is identical in both directions. In each round, all nodes exchange their distance vectors with their respective neighbors. Then each node updates their distance vectors. In between two rounds, any change in cost of a link will cause the two incident nodes to change only that entry in their distance vectors.

The cost of link N2-N3 reduces to 2 (in both directions). After the next round of updates, what will be the new distance vector at node N3?

A. (3, 2, 0, 2, 5)
B. (3, 2, 0, 2, 6)
C. (7, 2, 0, 2, 5)
D. (7, 2, 0, 2, 6)
questions.to_find: New distance vector at node N3 after one round of updates following the cost change of link N2-N3 to 2

understand.plain: Five nodes N1-N5 are connected with various link costs. After the network stabilizes, each node's distance vector is given. Then the cost of link N2-N3 drops from 6 to 2. After exactly ONE more round of distance vector exchange and updates, we need to find N3's new distance vector. In this one round, N3 will receive the current distance vectors from its neighbors (N1, N2, N4, N5 depending on topology) and compute new minimum distances.

keywords.explain: A table at each node listing the minimum known cost to reach every other node in the network.
keywords.example: N3's vector (7,6,0,2,6) means: cost to N1=7, N2=6, N3=0 (itself), N4=2, N5=6.

keywords.explain: Each node sends its current distance vector to neighbors, then each node recomputes its vector using the Bellman-Ford update rule.
keywords.example: N3 receives vectors from N1, N2, N4, N5 and picks the minimum cost path to each destination.

keywords.explain: When a link's cost changes, only the two incident nodes immediately update that direct-link entry in their vectors; propagation happens in subsequent rounds.
keywords.example: N2-N3 cost changes to 2: N2 updates its entry for N3 to 2, and N3 updates its entry for N2 to 2, BEFORE the next round.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 380 300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='190' cy='30' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>N1</text><circle cx='80' cy='130' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>N2</text><circle cx='300' cy='130' r='20' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>N3</text><circle cx='80' cy='250' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='256' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>N5</text><circle cx='300' cy='250' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='256' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>N4</text><line x1='175' y1='47' x2='95' y2='113' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='125' y='73' class='sub mut'>1</text><line x1='205' y1='47' x2='285' y2='113' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='253' y='73' class='sub mut'>7</text><line x1='80' y1='150' x2='80' y2='230' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='196' class='sub mut'>3</text><line x1='300' y1='150' x2='300' y2='230' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='310' y='196' class='sub mut'>2</text><line x1='100' y1='250' x2='280' y2='250' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='190' y='268' class='sub mut'>4</text><line x1='95' y1='143' x2='283' y2='143' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='190' y='135' class='tag' fill='#7F77DD'>2 (was 6)</text><line x1='97' y1='235' x2='287' y2='165' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='195' y='185' class='sub mut'>6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five nodes N1-N5. N1 at top connects to N2 (left) with cost 1 and N3 (right) with cost 7. N2 connects to N5 (bottom-left) with cost 3. N3 connects to N4 (bottom-right) with cost 2. N4 connects to N5 with cost 4. N2 connects to N5 with cost 3. The link N2-N3 is highlighted showing cost changed from 6 to 2. N3 is the node whose new distance vector we compute.

given.aim: Compute N3's new distance vector after one round of updates following the N2-N3 link cost reduction from 6 to 2

terms.meaning: Nodes directly connected to N3: N1 (cost 7), N2 (new cost 2), N4 (cost 2), N5 (cost 6)
terms.example: N3 exchanges distance vectors with N1, N2, N4, N5 in this round.
terms.connects: N3 will compute new distances using these neighbors' vectors plus the respective link costs

terms.meaning: Each node sends its vector from BEFORE this round starts. The only change before the round is that N2 and N3 update their direct-link entry for each other.
terms.example: N2's vector before this round: N2 updates N3-entry to 2, so N2 sends (1,0,2,7,3). N3 starts with direct entry for N2 = 2.
terms.connects: N3 uses these sent vectors plus link costs to find new minimums

terms.meaning: N3 computes: d_N3(dest) = min over neighbors n of {cost(N3,n) + d_n(dest)}
terms.connects: This gives the new distance vector entry for N3 to N1

terms.meaning: Before the round, N2 updates its entry for N3 to 2 (direct link cost change). N2's sent vector: (1,0,2,7,3)
terms.example: N2 to N3 was 6, now 2. Other entries remain: N2 to N1=1, N2 to N2=0, N2 to N4=7, N2 to N5=3.
terms.connects: This updated N2 vector is key to N3 getting better paths via N2 to distant nodes

given.plan: Identify all neighbors of N3 and their distance vectors at the start of this round. Note that N2's vector has been updated (N3-entry = 2) before sending. Apply Bellman-Ford: for each destination, N3 computes the minimum of (link_cost_to_neighbor + neighbor's distance to destination) over all neighbors.

steps.title: Identify N3's neighbors and their sent distance vectors
steps.formula_raw: Neighbors of N3: N1 (cost 7), N2 (cost 2 new), N4 (cost 2), N5 (cost 6)
steps.apply:
Vectors sent by neighbors (N2 updates its N3-entry before sending):
- N1 sends: (0,1,7,8,4)  [N1 does not yet know N2-N3 changed]
- N2 sends: (1,0,2,7,3)  [N2 updated N3-entry from 6 to 2]
- N4 sends: (8,7,2,0,4)  [N4 unchanged]
- N5 sends: (4,3,6,4,0)  [N5 unchanged]
Link costs from N3: to N1=7, to N2=2, to N4=2, to N5=6
steps.note: Between rounds, only the two incident nodes of a changed link update that specific entry. N2 updates its entry for N3 to 2, and N3 has the direct link cost to N2 = 2.

steps.title: Compute new N3 distance to N1
steps.formula_raw: d_N3(N1) = min{cost(N3,n) + d_n(N1)} for n in {N1, N2, N4, N5}
steps.note: Path N3->N2->N1 with cost 2+1=3 is now the best, thanks to the reduced N2-N3 link.

steps.title: Compute new N3 distance to N2
steps.formula_raw: d_N3(N2) = min{cost(N3,n) + d_n(N2)} for n in {N1, N2, N4, N5}
steps.note: Direct link N3-N2 now costs 2, which is the minimum.

steps.title: Compute new N3 distance to N3 (self) and N4
steps.note: Direct link N3-N4 costs 2, unchanged, remains the best path to N4.

steps.title: Compute new N3 distance to N5
steps.formula_raw: d_N3(N5) = min{cost(N3,n) + d_n(N5)} for n in {N1, N2, N4, N5}
steps.note: The N2-N3 cost reduction opens a cheaper path to N5 via N2. Old path was 6 (direct N3-N5), new best is 5 (via N2).

steps.title: Assemble new distance vector at N3
steps.note: All five entries computed: N1=3, N2=2, N3=0, N4=2, N5=5. This matches option A.

solution.result: New distance vector at N3 = (3, 2, 0, 2, 5). Answer: A. (3, 2, 0, 2, 5)

questions.formula_note: Bellman-Ford distance vector update: d_v(dest) = min over all neighbors u of {w(v,u) + d_u(dest)}, where w(v,u) is the direct link cost. After one round with N2-N3 reduced to 2, N3 benefits from cheaper paths via N2 to N1 and N5.
